title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Direct Mutual Fund vs Regular Mutual Fund: Which Option Fits Your Investing Style?
Mutual funds have become a go-to investment option for many people because they’re simple to start, professionally managed, and help spread your risk. However, before you begin, you face an important question: choosing between a direct mutual fund vs regular mutual fund.
It’s a bit like deciding whether to cook a meal yourself or order it from a trusted restaurant. Both choices can be good, but the experience, cost, and level of involvement vary. Let’s break it down and understand what really sets these two options apart.
Understanding Direct Mutual Fund Plans
A direct plan allows you to invest straight with the Asset Management Company, without any distributor or agent in the middle. You’re in charge of comparing schemes, tracking performance, and managing every step on your own.
Since there’s no intermediary involved, the expense ratio is lower, which means more of your money stays invested.
Understanding Regular Mutual Fund Plans
A regular plan is purchased through a middleman, such as a broker, advisor, or distributor, who guides you, suggests suitable schemes, and manages most of the formalities on your behalf.
Since their commission is built into the plan, the expense ratio is higher compared to direct options. Note: Only intermediaries with a valid AMFI Registration Number (ARN) are allowed to distribute mutual fund products
Why Mutual Funds Offer Both Direct and Regular Plans?
The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) introduced the two-plan system so investors can pick what suits them best:
Direct plan generally suits investors who are confident about researching funds and want a cost-effective way to build their portfolio.
A regular plan is ideal for new investors or anyone who prefers expert guidance instead of handling everything alone.
Direct vs Regular Mutual Fund: Key Differences Explained
Let us understand the main difference between a mutual fund regular vs direct:
Returns Impact Over Long Term
Direct plans usually grow faster because they have a lower expense ratio (TER). With no middleman involved, more of your money is directly invested, which pushes up the Net Asset Value (NAV) and your long-term returns.
Regular plans include distributor commissions, thus having a higher TER. This slight reduction in growth each year, and over long periods, compounds into a significant difference.
An example can provide a clear understanding: Suppose you invest ₹25,000 monthly in SIP for 30 years with an expected return 12%.
Regular Plan (TER ~1.5%) → Approx. ₹6.3 crore
Direct Plan (TER ~1%) → Approx. ₹7.8 crore
A 0.5% TER difference can create a ₹1.5 crore gap, just from lower costs.
NAV Difference Explained
The Net Asset Value of a mutual fund changes based on its total expenses. Because regular plans include distributor commissions within their higher expense ratio, more charges are deducted from the fund. As a result, the NAV of a regular plan ends up being lower than that of its direct counterpart, even though both invest in the same underlying portfolio.
Ease of Investment and Ongoing Management
When you pick a direct mutual fund, you handle everything from comparing funds to completing payments. This works well if you're comfortable making financial decisions and enjoy managing your own investments.
However, if you're new to mutual funds or prefer guidance, a regular plan may feel far smoother. The distributor can provide the necessary guidance, making the whole process easier.
Advantages of Investing Through Regular Mutual Fund Plans
Some reasons why investing in a regular plan is a good choice are covered below:
Personal GuidanceIf you're not confident about navigating mutual funds on your own, a trained distributor or advisor can be a big help. They break things down clearly, explain options, and guide you toward suitable choices.
Goal-Focused RecommendationsAdvisors usually begin by understanding what you want: a house, your child’s education, or retirement planning. Based on your goals and comfort with risk, they suggest funds that align with your long-term plans.
Ongoing Portfolio Check-ins
With a direct plan, you’re responsible for tracking your investments. In a regular plan, your mutual fund distributor (MFD) monitors your portfolio, alerts you about necessary changes, and helps you rebalance when required, making the process smoother for busy investors.
How to Identify Whether Your Mutual Fund is Direct or Regular?
Here are some simple ways to check if you’ve invested in a regular vs direct plan in mutual fund:
Checking the Fund Name
You can often identify the type of plan just by looking at the scheme name. Direct plans usually include the word “Direct” or sometimes “Dir.” If the label says “Regular” or “Reg,” or if no direct tag is visible, you’re most likely viewing a regular mutual fund option.
Reviewing the Expense Ratio (TER)
The expense ratio is another easy method. Regular plans cost more because they include distributor commissions, while direct plans have a lower TER. Always verify this number to know which option you’re choosing.
Using Online Platforms and Statements
Your account statements can also tell you which plan you hold. In the Advisor section of your Consolidated Account Statement, if you see an ARN number, it means the investment was made through a distributor (a regular plan). If it shows “Direct”, zero, or similar codes, it indicates a direct mutual fund.
Choosing Between Direct and Regular Mutual Fund Plans
Here’s how you may choose between a direct mutual fund vs regular mutual fund:
Suitable for Guidance-Seeking Investors
If you’re not confident about researching funds, comparing options, or tracking markets, a regular plan may serve you better. An experienced distributor or advisor can guide you, explain choices, and help you align investments with your goals.
When Combining Both Plans Makes Sense
If you understand the basics but still need occasional support, you can mix both options. Use direct plans for funds you’re comfortable managing yourself, and choose regular plans when you need expert input, providing flexibility without depending entirely on an intermediary.
How to Invest in Direct or Regular Mutual Fund Plans?
To start investing, you begin with the mandatory step of completing your KYC, which verifies your identity and address. Then choose whether you want to go directly or use an intermediary. For direct plans, you can invest online through the mutual fund’s own website, the registrar portals like MFU, or submit a physical form to the fund house. For regular plans, you can also invest through a registered distributor, broker, or bank channel.
Switching from a Regular to a Direct Mutual Fund Plan
Moving from a regular mutual fund plan to its direct version is simple. Here’s how it usually works:
Step 1: Sign in to your mutual fund portal or the platform where you hold your investment.Step 2: Go to your list of existing schemes and pick the regular plan you want to shift out of.Step 3: Choose the ‘Convert’ or ‘Switch’ option and select the direct version of the same scheme.Step 4: Enter the switch amount, review any exit load or tax implications, and submit your request.
Your updated units will come into effect within a few working days.
Taxation and Exit Load Considerations
When you transfer from a regular plan to its direct version, the transaction is treated as a sale and a fresh purchase. Your regular-plan units will be redeemed, and new units will be issued under the direct plan. If your investment has appreciated, this redemption will attract capital gains tax. You may also bear an exit load if you switch before the required holding period ends.
Short-term capital gains apply to equity funds if you exit within one year, and ELSS units can’t be shifted at all until the three-year lock-in is over. So, before switching, it’s smart to check taxes, timing, and charges.
Other Investment Alternatives to Mutual Funds
Apart from mutual funds, there are various investment avenues to explore. Investing in direct equity, fixed deposits, bonds, government schemes, or Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs). If you want insurance coverage and wealth accumulation, you may opt for ULIPs.
Key Takeaway
Direct mutual fund vs regular mutual funds mainly differ in cost; direct plans have lower expense ratios and higher NAVs, leading to better long-term returns.
Direct plans suit investors who can research, compare schemes, and track portfolios on their own.
Regular plans work well for beginners who need personalised advice, goal planning, and ongoing portfolio reviews.
The long-term return gap can be significant due to commission-linked expenses in regular plans.
You can check if your mutual fund is regular or direct from its fund name, expense ratio, or statements.
You can switch from regular to direct, but it may trigger capital gains tax or exit loads.
Conclusion
It’s quite clear now how direct mutual fund vs regular mutual fund options differ and who they suit. Direct plans help you keep more of your returns because there’s no commission involved, making them appealing for investors who’re comfortable managing things on their own. However, if you are a beginner or do not closely follow markets, a regular plan with an advisor can offer much-needed support.
Regardless of the route you choose, remember that mutual funds carry market risk. Stay informed, review your investments regularly, and choose the path that matches your confidence, time, and financial goals.
FAQs
Are direct mutual funds riskier than regular mutual funds?
Mutual funds, whether regular or direct, carry market risk. While direct plans give higher returns, they may be risky if you select the wrong scheme.
Is NAV always higher in direct mutual fund plans?
As direct mutual funds have a lower expense ratio, their NAV is higher than that of the regular counterpart.
Does switching from regular to direct trigger capital gains tax?
Since switching from a regular to a direct mutual fund is considered a fresh sale and purchase, it may attract capital gains tax on the positive growth of the investment.
Can I start with a regular plan and later switch to direct?
Switching from the regular plan is possible, but it will attract exit load and tax. So, you should plan it only after checking taxes, timing, and charges.
Can I mix direct and regular mutual funds in one portfolio?
Yes, you can buy direct mutual funds when you’re confident, and buy another regular plan via an intermediary if you need expert advice.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
full_pdf_text:
Direct Mutual Fund vs Regular Mutual Fund: Which Option Fits Your Investing Style?
Mutual funds have become a go-to investment option for many people because they’re simple to start, professionally managed, and help spread your risk. However, before you begin, you face an important question: choosing between a direct mutual fund vs regular mutual fund.
It’s a bit like deciding whether to cook a meal yourself or order it from a trusted restaurant. Both choices can be good, but the experience, cost, and level of involvement vary. Let’s break it down and understand what really sets these two options apart.
Understanding Direct Mutual Fund Plans
A direct plan allows you to invest straight with the Asset Management Company, without any distributor or agent in the middle. You’re in charge of comparing schemes, tracking performance, and managing every step on your own.
Since there’s no intermediary involved, the expense ratio is lower.
Understanding Regular Mutual Fund Plans
A regular plan is purchased through a middleman, such as a broker, advisor, or distributor, who guides you, suggests suitable schemes, and manages most of the formalities on your behalf.
Since their commission is built into the plan, the expense ratio is higher compared to direct options. Note: Only intermediaries with a valid AMFI Registration Number (ARN) are allowed to distribute mutual fund products
Why Mutual Funds Offer Both Direct and Regular Plans?
The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) introduced the two-plan system so investors can pick what suits them best:
1. Direct plan generally suits investors who are confident about researching funds and want a
cost-effective way to build their portfolio. 2. A regular plan is ideal for new investors or anyone who prefers expert guidance instead of
handling everything alone.
Direct vs Regular Mutual Fund: Key Differences Explained
Let us understand the main difference between a mutual fund regular vs direct:
1. Returns Impact Over Long Term
Direct plans usually grow faster because they have a lower expense ratio (TER). With no middleman involved, more of your money is directly invested1, which pushes up the Net Asset Value (NAV) since the total expense ratio is lower. Regular plans include distributor commissions, thus having a higher TER. An example can provide a clear understanding: Suppose you invest ₹25,000 monthly in SIP for 30 years with an expected return 12%.
o Regular Plan (TER ~1.5%) → Approx. ₹6.3 crore o Direct Plan (TER ~1%) → Approx. ₹7.8 crore A 0.5% reduction in TER increases the effective return by the same margin each year. Over long durations, this minor annual difference compounds significantly, resulting in a total corpus difference of about ₹1.5 crore on a ₹25,000 monthly SIP over 30 years. 2. NAV Difference Explained
The Net Asset Value of a mutual fund changes affected by the total expenses charged on the scheme. Since regular plans include distributor commissions within their expense ratio, more charges are deducted from the fund. As a result, even though direct and regular plans invest in the exact same underlying portfolio, the NAV of a regular plan will usually be lower than that of its direct plan counterpart. 3. Ease of Investment and Ongoing Management
When you pick a direct mutual fund, you handle everything from comparing funds to completing payments. This works well if you're comfortable making financial decisions and enjoy managing your own investments.
However, if you're new to mutual funds or prefer guidance, a regular plan may feel far smoother. The distributor can provide the necessary guidance, making the whole process easier.
Advantages of Investing Through Regular Mutual Fund Plans
Some of the benefits of regular mutual funds are covered below1:
1. Personal Guidance
If you're not confident about navigating mutual funds on your own, a trained distributor or advisor can be a big help. They break things down clearly, explain options, and guide you toward suitable choices. 2. Goal-Focused Recommendations
Advisors usually begin by understanding what you want: a house, your child’s education, or retirement planning. Based on your goals and comfort with risk, they suggest funds that align with your long-term plans. 3. Ongoing Portfolio Check-ins
With a direct plan, you’re responsible for tracking your investments. In a regular plan, your mutual fund distributor (MFD) monitors your portfolio, alerts you about necessary changes, and helps you rebalance when required, making the process smoother for busy investors.
Advantages of investing through direct mutual funds
Direct mutual funds also have their advantages for investors. Some of these advantages are as follows –
 Low expense ratios
In the case of direct funds, there are no intermediary fees included in the total expenses of the fund. As such, the expense ratio tends to be low which increases the NAV and your returns.
 Higher return potential in the long run The lower expense ratio of direct funds helps in boosting returns. Over time, even a small difference in returns can compound and grow into a considerable amount.  Transparency As you invest directly, you have complete control in the choice of fund you want and how the chosen fund is performing. This gives you complete transparency.
How to Identify Whether Your Mutual Fund is Direct or Regular?
Here are some simple ways to check if you’ve invested in a regular vs direct plan in mutual fund:
1. Checking the Fund Name
You can often identify the type of plan just by looking at the scheme name. Direct plans usually include the word “Direct” or sometimes “Dir.” If the label says “Regular” or “Reg,” or if no direct tag is visible, you’re most likely viewing a regular mutual fund option. 2. Reviewing the Expense Ratio (TER)
The expense ratio is another easy method. Regular plans have a higher expense ratio because they include distributor commissions, while direct plans have a lower TER. Always verify this number to know which option you’re choosing. 3. Using Online Platforms and Statements
Your account statements can also tell you which plan you hold. In the Advisor section of your Consolidated Account Statement, if you see an ARN number, it means the investment was made through a distributor (a regular plan). If it shows “Direct”, zero, or similar codes, it indicates a direct mutual fund.
Choosing Between Direct and Regular Mutual Fund Plans
Here’s how you may choose between a direct mutual fund vs regular mutual fund:
1. Suitable for Guidance-Seeking Investors
If you’re not confident about researching funds, comparing options, or tracking markets, a regular plan may serve you better. An experienced distributor or advisor can guide you, explain choices, and help you align investments with your goals. 2. When a Hybrid Approach Works Best
If you understand the basics but still need occasional support, you can use a hybrid approach i.e. use direct plans for funds you’re comfortable managing yourself, and choose regular plans when you need expert input, providing flexibility without depending entirely on an intermediary.
How to Invest in Direct or Regular Mutual Fund Plans?
You can invest online through the mutual fund’s own website, the registrar portals like MFU, or submit a physical form to the fund house2. You can also invest through a registered distributor, broker, or bank channel2.
Switching from a Regular to a Direct Mutual Fund Plan
Moving from a regular mutual fund plan to its direct version is simple. Here’s how it usually works:
Step 1: Sign in to your mutual fund portal or the platform where you hold your investment. Step 2: Go to your list of existing schemes and pick the regular plan you want to shift out of. Step 3: Choose the ‘Convert’ or ‘Switch’ option and select the direct version of the same scheme. Step 4: Enter the switch amount, review any exit load or tax implications, and submit your request.
Your updated units will come into effect within a few working days.
Taxation and Exit Load Considerations3
When you transfer from a regular plan to its direct version, the transaction is treated as a sale and a fresh purchase. Your regular-plan units will be redeemed, and new units will be issued under the direct plan. If your investment has appreciated, this redemption will attract capital gains tax. You may also bear an exit load if you switch before the required holding period ends.
Short-term capital gains apply to equity funds if you exit within one year, and ELSS units can’t be shifted at all until the three-year lock-in is over. So, before switching, it’s smart to check taxes, timing, and charges.
Key Takeaway
1. Direct mutual fund vs regular mutual funds mainly differ in cost; direct plans have lower
expense ratios and higher NAVs, leading to better long-term returns. 2. Direct plans suit investors who can research, compare schemes, and track portfolios on their
own. 3. Regular plans work well for beginners who need personalised advice, goal planning, and
ongoing portfolio reviews. 4. The long-term return gap can be significant due to commission-linked expenses in regular
plans. 5. You can check if your mutual fund is regular or direct from its fund name, expense ratio, or
statements. 6. You can switch from regular to direct, but it may trigger capital gains tax or exit loads.
Conclusion
It’s quite clear now how direct mutual fund vs regular mutual fund options differ and who they suit. Direct plans are appealing for those investors who’re comfortable managing their investments on their own. However, if you are a beginner or do not closely follow markets, a regular plan with an advisor can offer much-needed support.
Regardless of the route you choose, remember that mutual funds carry market risk. Stay informed, review your investments regularly, and choose the path that matches your confidence, time, and financial goals.
FAQs
1. Are direct mutual funds riskier than regular mutual funds?
Mutual funds, whether regular or direct, carry market risk. While direct plans give higher returns, they may be risky if you select the wrong scheme. 2. Is NAV always higher in direct mutual fund plans?
As direct mutual funds have a lower expense ratio, their NAV is higher than that of the regular counterpart. 3. Does switching from regular to direct trigger capital gains tax?
Since switching from a regular to a direct mutual fund is considered a fresh sale and purchase, it may attract capital gains tax on the positive growth of the investment. 4. Can I start with a regular plan and later switch to direct?
Switching from the regular plan is possible, but it will attract exit load and tax. So, you should plan it only after checking taxes, timing, and charges. 5. Can I mix direct and regular mutual funds in one portfolio?
Yes, you can buy direct mutual funds when you’re confident, and buy another regular plan via an intermediary if you need expert advice.
Disclaimer

While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Source

The long-term return gap can be significant due to commission-linked expenses in regular
plans. 5.
You can check if your mutual fund is regular or direct from its fund name, expense ratio, or
statements. 6.

changes.word_original: Direct plans help you keep more of your returns because there’s no commission involved, making them appealing for investors who’re comfortable managing things on their own.
changes.pdf_final: Direct plans are appealing for those investors who’re comfortable managing their investments on their own.

changes.word_original: While direct plans give higher returns, they may be risky if you select the wrong scheme.
changes.pdf_final: While direct plans give higher returns, they may be risky if you select the wrong scheme. 2.

changes.word_original: As direct mutual funds have a lower expense ratio, their NAV is higher than that of the regular counterpart.
changes.pdf_final: As direct mutual funds have a lower expense ratio, their NAV is higher than that of the regular counterpart. 3.

changes.word_original: Since switching from a regular to a direct mutual fund is considered a fresh sale and purchase, it may attract capital gains tax on the positive growth of the investment.
changes.pdf_final: Since switching from a regular to a direct mutual fund is considered a fresh sale and purchase, it may attract capital gains tax on the positive growth of the investment. 4.

changes.word_original: So, you should plan it only after checking taxes, timing, and charges.
changes.pdf_final: So, you should plan it only after checking taxes, timing, and charges. 5.
